title: Light Sources, Field Measurement, and Consumables
narrationScript: You can magnetize a part perfectly and still miss the crack, because in magnetic particle testing the last link in the chain is light. Treat light as a calibrated variable, just like amperage. For visible particles, black or red against the surface, typical procedure values require at least one hundred footcandles of white light at the inspection surface, which is roughly one thousand lux. Not at the lamp. At the surface you are reading. For fluorescent particles the rules flip. The particles glow green yellow under ultraviolet light in the U V A band, centered around three hundred sixty five nanometers, and the eye needs darkness for that glow to stand out. Typical requirements are at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter of U V A intensity at the surface, with ambient white light held to two footcandles or less, which is about twenty lux, inside the booth or under the hood. Both numbers are verified with meters, not eyeballs. A radiometer measures U V A intensity, and a white light meter measures footcandles or lux. Procedures commonly check the lamp at fifteen inches, or at the actual working distance, and on a set frequency, because bulbs age, filters crack, and intensity drifts down long before the lamp visibly dies. Mercury vapor lamps need several minutes of warm up before they reach full output. L E D ultraviolet lamps reach intensity almost instantly but must be certified for the work, because beam profile and emission band matter. And give your own eyes time. When you step into a darkened booth, pause and let them adapt before you start reading, at least a minute by common practice. Next, the field itself. Amperage on a meter proves current flowed. It does not prove field direction or adequacy at the surface. For direction, the classic tool is the pie gauge, an octagon of furnace brazed steel segments placed face down on the part during the shot. Particle lines form across the segment boundaries that lie at an angle to the flux, showing you the field direction on the spot. It is quick and qualitative, surface only. The better tool for proving a technique is the artificial flaw shim, often called a Q Q I, a thin steel foil with a precise etched slot, taped tightly to the part with the flaw side against the surface. When the field is adequate and properly oriented, the etched flaw shows as a particle indication, and on multidirectional equipment shims oriented for each direction prove the balance. For field strength numbers, the Hall effect gaussmeter measures the tangential field at the surface, and procedures that use it commonly target a tangential field on the order of thirty to sixty gauss during the shot. The pocket field indicator, the little pointer instrument from the demagnetization lesson, rounds out the kit for residual field checks. Now the consumables. Visible particles work in ordinary shop light and suit dirty field conditions. Fluorescent particles, used in a darkened booth, deliver far higher contrast and find finer flaws, which is why they dominate bench work. Dry powder versus wet bath follows the rule from earlier in this module. Dry for rough, hot, field work and near surface targets with half wave current. Wet for fine flaws and full coverage on machined parts. Wet baths need management because they recirculate. The carrier is either conditioned water, with wetting agents, corrosion inhibitor, and antifoam additives, or a low viscosity oil with a controlled flash point. Concentration is checked with a settling test. Fill a pear shaped centrifuge tube from the running system, let it settle, commonly thirty minutes for water baths and longer for oil, and read the settled solids. Typical procedure values are one point two to two point four milliliters per hundred milliliters for visible particles, and zero point one to zero point four for fluorescent. Lean baths give faint indications. Rich baths bury them in background. While you are at the tube, look at the settled layer itself. Dirt, lint, and fluorescent background in the carrier mean the bath is contaminated and due for replacement, no matter what the volume reads. For portable work, the same materials come packaged as aerosol cans of premixed suspension, powder bulbs, and pre measured concentrate kits, with shelf life and batch certifications to track. Light verified with a meter, field proven with a shim, bath checked with a tube. Those three habits at the start of every shift are what make the rest of your shift mean anything.
imagePrompt: Inside a darkened inspection booth, a technician holds a digital radiometer flat against a steel gear under the violet glow of a UV-A lamp, with a pear-shaped centrifuge tube of settled fluorescent bath in a stand nearby.

sections.heading: Light Is a Calibrated Variable
sections.body:
Magnetize perfectly and you can still miss the crack if the light is wrong — so MT procedures treat illumination exactly like amperage: specified, measured, recorded.

- **White light (9.8.1).** Visible (black/red) particle examinations typically require **at least 100 footcandles (≈ 1,000 lux) at the examination surface** — measured where you read indications, not at the lamp.
- **UV-A light (9.8.2).** Fluorescent particles excite under UV-A (centered near **365 nm**) and emit green-yellow light. Typical procedure values: **UV-A intensity ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² at the part surface**, verified with a radiometer.
- **Ambient light (9.8.3).** Fluorescent contrast lives or dies on darkness: **ambient white light ≤ 2 fc (≈ 20 lux)** in the booth or under the inspection hood. A cracked curtain seam or a propped door quietly destroys sensitivity.

These three numbers — 100 fc, 1,000 µW/cm², 2 fc — frame nearly every lighting decision an MT technician makes. Your written procedure governs the exact values and check frequency.

sections.caption: Fluorescent inspection booth with typical light limits: UV-A at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface and ambient white light no more than 2 footcandles.

sections.heading: Measuring Light (9.8.4)
sections.body:
Two meters, both calibrated on a schedule:

- **Radiometer** — reads UV-A intensity in **microwatts per square centimeter (µW/cm²)**, with the sensor placed at the examination surface. Procedures commonly verify lamp output at **15 in.** or at the actual working distance, at shift start and on a set frequency.
- **White-light (lux/footcandle) meter** — verifies both the ≥ 100 fc visible-method minimum and the ≤ 2 fc ambient maximum in the booth.

Why so much metering? Because light sources drift. **Mercury-vapor UV lamps need several minutes of warm-up** to reach full output, and their intensity decays as bulbs and filters age — long before the lamp visibly dies. **LED UV-A lamps** reach intensity almost instantly but must be **certified for MT use**, since beam profile, emission wavelength, and stability all affect indication visibility. Cracked or missing UV filters are a reject condition for the lamp, not a cosmetic flaw.

Finally, meter your own eyes: entering a darkened booth, **pause and let your vision dark-adapt** — commonly at least one minute by typical practice — before reading parts.

sections.heading: Field Measurement Devices (9.9): Direction and Strength
sections.body:
The ammeter proves current flowed; it proves nothing about **field direction or adequacy at the surface**. That takes dedicated devices.

**Field direction (9.9.1):**

- **Pie (octagon) gauge** — eight low-carbon steel segments furnace-brazed together, placed face down on the part during the shot. Particles collect along the segment boundaries that cross the flux, revealing field direction on the spot. Quick and qualitative — it samples the field at the surface where it sits, nothing deeper.
- **Artificial-flaw shims (QQIs)** — thin steel foils with precisely etched slots, taped **flaw-side against the part** so the etched flaw experiences the part's surface field. An adequate, properly oriented field produces a crisp particle indication of the etched pattern. Shims oriented for each direction are how multidirectional balance is proven.

**Field strength (9.9.2):**

- **Hall-effect gaussmeter** — a calibrated probe reading the **tangential field at the surface**; procedures that measure it commonly target on the order of **30–60 gauss** during magnetization.
- **Pocket field indicator** — the pointer instrument used for residual-field verification after demagnetization.

sections.caption: Field verification on the part: a pie gauge shows flux direction, a QQI shim proves adequacy and orientation, and a Hall-effect probe reads tangential field strength.

sections.heading: Materials (9.10): Visible, Fluorescent, Wet, Dry, Portable
sections.body:
**Visible particles (9.10.1)** — black or red contrast media read under ordinary white light (≥ 100 fc). They tolerate daylight, dirt, and field conditions, and need no booth — the structural and pipeline standby.

**Fluorescent particles (9.10.2)** — glow green-yellow under UV-A in a darkened booth. The dark-background contrast is dramatically higher, which finds finer flaws; this is why bench and aerospace work is overwhelmingly fluorescent.

**Wet versus dry (9.10.3)** — the rule from the first lesson of this module: **dry powder** for rough, hot, field work and near-surface targets with HWDC; **wet suspension** for fine surface flaws and complete coverage on machined parts.

**Portable materials (9.10.5)** — the same chemistry packaged for the truck: **aerosol cans** of premixed visible or fluorescent suspension, powder bulbs and shakers, and pre-measured concentrate kits. Track shelf life and batch certifications, shake aerosols thoroughly, and remember a can sprayed past its useful life is an unverified bath you cannot run a settling test on — when in doubt, a new can is cheaper than a missed crack.

sections.caption: Settling test: bath drawn from the running system settles in a graduated centrifuge tube — typical limits 1.2–2.4 mL/100 mL visible, 0.1–0.4 mL/100 mL fluorescent.

sections.heading: Bath Properties and Concentration (9.10.4)
sections.body:
A recirculating bath is a managed consumable with two halves: carrier and particles.

**Carrier.** Either **conditioned water** — with wetting agents so the bath sheets evenly over the surface, corrosion inhibitors to protect parts and tank, and antifoam — or a **low-viscosity light oil** with a controlled flash point for parts that cannot tolerate water. A water bath that beads up instead of wetting the part is telling you the conditioning is gone.

**Concentration.** Checked by the **settling test**: fill a pear-shaped, graduated centrifuge tube from the running system (after thorough agitation), let it settle — commonly **30 minutes for water baths**, longer for oil — and read the settled solids. Typical procedure values: **visible 1.2–2.4 mL per 100 mL; fluorescent 0.1–0.4 mL per 100 mL**. Lean baths produce faint indications; rich baths bury indications in background fluorescence.

**Condition.** Read the tube itself: stratified dirt or lint above the particles, dull or mottled settled color, or fluorescent glow in the carrier liquid means contamination or particle breakdown — **replace the bath** regardless of the volume reading. Shift-start checks of concentration and condition are standard practice.

sections.title: UV-A and bath safety
sections.body: Never look into a UV-A lamp or aim it at anyone's eyes — UV exposure to eyes and skin accumulates, so work with **intact filters** (a cracked filter can pass hazardous shorter-wavelength UV and white light that kills contrast) and keep skin covered during long booth sessions. Oil-carrier baths are **combustible**: respect the flash point, keep ignition sources away from tanks and overspray, and ventilate. Hot parts plus suspension can generate vapor and steam — let temperature and your procedure, not the schedule, decide when the part gets wet.

figures.title: Field direction and strength verification devices

interactive.title: Shift-Start Checks: In or Out of Tolerance?
interactive.intro: You are opening the fluorescent bench station for the day shift. Classify each meter reading or settling-tube result against typical procedure limits (your written procedure governs the real numbers).
buckets: Out of tolerance — correct before testing

items.label: Radiometer: 1,250 µW/cm² UV-A at the part surface
items.why: Typical procedures require at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface — 1,250 clears the minimum.

items.label: Radiometer: 850 µW/cm² after full lamp warm-up
items.why: Below the typical 1,000 µW/cm² minimum — clean or replace the bulb/filter and re-verify before inspecting.

items.label: Booth ambient white light: 1.5 fc
items.why: Ambient light must typically be 2 fc or less for fluorescent work; 1.5 fc preserves contrast.

items.label: Booth ambient white light: 6 fc with the curtain gapped open
items.why: Three times the typical 2 fc maximum — close the curtain and re-measure; ambient white light washes out fluorescent indications.

items.label: Fluorescent bath settles 0.25 mL per 100 mL
items.why: Inside the typical 0.1–0.4 mL/100 mL fluorescent range.

items.label: Fluorescent bath settles 0.7 mL per 100 mL
items.why: Over-concentrated — background fluorescence will mask fine indications; dilute and re-test.

items.label: Visible bath settles 1.5 mL per 100 mL
items.why: Within the typical 1.2–2.4 mL/100 mL range for visible particles.

items.label: Settling tube shows a thick layer of lint and dirt above the particles
items.why: Contamination is a replace-the-bath condition regardless of the concentration reading.

items.label: White light at the surface for a visible-particle check: 60 fc
items.why: Visible examinations typically require at least 100 fc at the surface — add or reposition lighting and re-measure.

checkQuestions.question: Typical lighting requirements for a fluorescent MT examination are:
options: UV-A ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface with ambient white light ≤ 2 fc
UV-A ≥ 100 µW/cm² at the lamp face with ambient ≤ 20 fc
White light ≥ 100 fc at the surface with no UV requirement
UV-A ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² measured anywhere in the booth
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent work typically needs at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A measured at the examination surface and a darkened booth at 2 fc or less of ambient white light — both verified by meter.

checkQuestions.question: A pie gauge placed on a part during the shot shows particle lines forming across some segment boundaries. This tells the technician:
options: The exact tangential field strength in gauss
The approximate direction of the field at the surface where the gauge sits
The depth of penetration of the field
The bath concentration is adequate
checkQuestions.explanation: The pie gauge is a quick, qualitative direction indicator: indications form on boundaries crossing the flux, revealing field direction at the surface — it measures neither strength in numbers nor depth.

checkQuestions.question: The correct way to use a QQI artificial-flaw shim is:
options: Held loosely on the surface, etched side up, after the shot
Taped tightly to the part with the etched flaw side against the surface, during magnetization
Placed inside the coil with no part present
Glued to the UV lamp filter as a reference mark
checkQuestions.explanation: The QQI's etched flaw must experience the part's surface field, so it is taped flaw-side down in intimate contact; a crisp indication of the etched pattern proves field adequacy and orientation.

checkQuestions.question: When a procedure verifies field strength with a Hall-effect gaussmeter, the measured value is the:
options: Tangential field at the part surface, commonly targeted around 30–60 G
Current density inside the part, in amperes per inch
Residual field one hour after the shot
UV-A intensity converted to gauss
checkQuestions.explanation: Hall-probe verification reads the tangential surface field during magnetization, with procedures commonly targeting on the order of 30–60 gauss — amperage and lighting are separate measurements entirely.

checkQuestions.question: A 30-minute settling test on the fluorescent bench bath reads 0.05 mL per 100 mL. The likely consequence and correct action are:
options: Background fluorescence will mask flaws; dilute the bath
Indications will be faint or missed; add particle concentrate and re-test
No effect; fluorescent baths have no minimum
The carrier is contaminated; ignore the reading
checkQuestions.explanation: 0.05 mL/100 mL is below the typical 0.1–0.4 fluorescent range — a lean bath produces weak, dim indications, so concentrate is added and the settling test repeated before inspecting.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §9.8 (light sources and measurement 9.8.1–9.8.4)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §9.9 (field measurement devices 9.9.1–9.9.2)
Practices such as ASTM E1444, ASTM E709, and ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (lighting, QQI, and bath concentration values — general practice)
